Paul begins this chapter with urgency: he is reminding the Corinthians of the gospel they received and in which they stand, but his tone suggests they are in danger of losing their footing. The foundation is not complicated—Christ died for sins, was buried, was raised on the third day, and appeared to hundreds of witnesses, including to Paul himself. This is not speculation or interpretation. This is what Paul received and passed on as of first importance. And here is the weight of it: if you hold firmly to this word, you are saved. If you do not, you have believed in vain. The gospel is not one truth among many. It is the singular reality upon which everything else rests. Paul stakes his own apostolic authority on this—a man who once persecuted the church now works harder than all the other apostles, not because of his own strength, but because God's grace toward him was not wasted. He knows what he is talking about.

Yet some Corinthians are saying there is no resurrection of the dead, and Paul responds with devastating logic. If there is no resurrection, then Christ was not raised. If Christ was not raised, then the apostles are liars bearing false witness about God. Christian preaching is worthless. Christian faith is futile. The dead in Christ have perished. And if our hope in Christ is only for this life, we deserve only pity. But then Paul stops the hypothetical collapse and declares the reality: Christ has been raised from the dead. He is the firstfruits—the guarantee and the pattern of what is to come. Death came through Adam; resurrection comes through Christ. In Adam, all die. In Christ, all will be made alive. This is not metaphor. This is the reversal of human history, the undoing of the Fall itself, accomplished through the God-man who rose on the third day.

The resurrection is not abstract theology—it demands a reorientation of your entire life. If Christ is raised, then those who endure now, who sacrifice now, who face danger now, are not fools. They are people living in light of what is actually true. Paul then answers the objection about how resurrection works by pointing to nature itself: a seed must die before it produces a body. God gives each thing its own body. Similarly, your resurrection body will be real—continuous with who you are—yet transformed: imperishable instead of perishable, glorious instead of shameful, powerful instead of weak, spiritual instead of merely natural. You will bear the image of the heavenly man as surely as you now bear the image of the earthly man.

Here is the final word: in an instant, at the last trumpet, the dead will be raised imperishable and we will be changed. The sting of death is sin, but thanks be to God, who gives us the victory through Jesus Christ. This is not comfort without cost. It is the declaration of something that has already begun in Christ's resurrection and will be completed when He returns. You now live in that overlap—you have the firstfruits of the Spirit, but you await the full harvest. Therefore, be steadfast, unmovable, always abounding in the work of the Lord, knowing that your labor is not in vain. Every act of faithfulness, every act of sacrifice, every moment of obedience done in Christ—it will not be lost. It will be redeemed and made eternal.
